text.title: Thomas-Kilmann five conflict modes
text.summary: Five conflict modes on two axes (assertiveness and cooperativeness); mode selection matters more than message quality — using Competing when Collaborating is possible produces short-term compliance and long-term resentment.

rule.statement: Select the conflict mode before drafting any message. The wrong mode undermines even a perfectly worded NVC script.
rule.rationale: Mode sets the intent (I win / we both win / no one wins). A Collaborating script delivered in a Competing posture reads as manipulative.

list.item: Unpopular decision that is clearly correct and must be made
Self-protection against someone exploiting cooperative behavior
Routine issues where relationship preservation matters
You might be wrong — competing with a flawed position destroys credibility
The issue is important and both parties have valid interests
Long-term relationship requires genuine buy-in, not compliance
Time is available to explore solutions together
Trivial matters where the effort is disproportionate
No trust exists between parties — collaboration requires a minimum of good faith
Issue is moderately important, parties have equal power, and time is limited
Temporary settlement needed while a better solution is developed
Trivial issue not worth the relationship cost
Emotions are too high — a cooling-off period is needed before productive dialogue
Issue is important and will not resolve itself — avoidance becomes accumulation
You are factually wrong — accommodation is the honest response
The issue matters more to them than to you; preserving goodwill is the priority
Core values or principles are at stake
You have accommodated repeatedly in this relationship — the pattern itself becomes the problem
